data.title: Versigent Spin-Off And Debt Tender Could Be A Game Changer For Aptiv (APTV)
data.description:

  • By pairing the Versigent spin-off with new bond issuances and a sizable tender offer, Aptiv is actively reshaping its balance sheet and refocusing on higher-growth electronics and software-oriented operations.

To own Aptiv today, you need to believe that separating Versigent and concentrating on electronics, software and ADAS can outweigh near term macro, China and execution risks. The Versigent spin off, paired with the US$1.35 billion note tender and new bond issues, directly affects Aptiv’s balance sheet, but does not fundamentally change that the key near term catalyst is cleaner, higher margin earnings from the remaining portfolio, while leverage and China exposure stay central risks.

The most relevant recent announcement here is the Versigent spin off itself, which is tied to a US$1.70 billion special dividend back to Aptiv. That cash, along with the new 2031 and 2034 senior notes, gives the company room to retire older debt and potentially support its focus on higher value ADAS and software programs. Advertisement

Approach 1: Chemed Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a business may generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the entire company could be worth now.

For Chemed, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is $329.0 million. Approach 1: Globus Medical Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes Globus Medical's expected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now in dollar terms. It is essentially asking what a stream of future cash flows is worth in today's money.

For Globus Medical, the model starts with last twelve months free cash flow of about $603.6 million. Analysts provide estimates out to 2027, including projected free cash flow of $510.85 million in 2027, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates cash flows further using its own growth assumptions.

The ten year projections range from $395.6 million in 2026 to $663.9 million in 2035, with each year discounted back to today. Adding these discounted values and a terminal value gives an estimated intrinsic value of $83.88 per share using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model.

Set against the recent share price of $84.77, the DCF suggests Globus Medical is about 1.1% overvalued, which is effectively in line with the current market pricing.

Approach 2: Globus Medical Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Globus Medical, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger earnings growth or see lower risk, and look for a lower P/E when growth is more modest or risks feel higher.

Globus Medical currently trades on a P/E of 21.32x. That sits below the Medical Equipment industry average P/E of 26.81x, and also below the peer group average of 48.39x. data.title: PolyPeptide Group (SWX:PPGN) Loss Widens To €0.80 EPS Challenging Turnaround Narratives
data.description:
PolyPeptide Group (SWX:PPGN) has just posted its FY 2025 first half results, reporting revenue of €167.1 million and a loss per share of €0.80, alongside net income excluding extra items of a €26.5 million loss. Over recent periods the company’s revenue moved from €135.0 million in the first half of 2024 to €201.7 million in the second half of 2024 and then to €167.1 million in the first half of 2025. EPS over the same periods shifted from a €0.35 loss to a €0.24 loss and then to a €0.80 loss, leaving investors focused on how these trends affect margins and the company’s path back to healthier profitability.

See our full analysis for PolyPeptide Group.

With the latest numbers now available, the next step is to consider how this earnings profile aligns with prevailing narratives around PolyPeptide Group’s growth potential, risk profile, and the timing of any margin recovery.

See what the community is saying about PolyPeptide Group

Advertisement

Losses Widen To €26.5 Million On €167.1 Million In Sales

  • For the first half of 2025, PolyPeptide generated €167.1 million in revenue and reported a net loss excluding extra items of €26.5 million, compared with a €8.2 million loss on €201.7 million of revenue in the second half of 2024.
  • Bears argue that this loss profile, with trailing 12 month net income excluding extra items at a €34.7 million loss, fits a story where higher capacity and exposure to metabolics do not yet translate into stronger profitability.
    • This view points to five year losses having grown at about 66.2% a year and earnings quality being described as negative, which aligns with the most recent half year EPS loss of €0.80.
    • At the same time, consensus revenue growth forecasts of around 16.7% a year highlight the core tension that revenue expectations are strong while actual earnings are still firmly in loss making territory.

Forecasted 51.5% Earnings Growth Versus Current €34.7 Million TTM Loss

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, PolyPeptide recorded €368.8 million in revenue and a €34.7 million net loss excluding extra items, while forecasts point to earnings expanding around 51.5% a year with an expectation of reaching profitability within three years.
  • Consensus narrative suggests that exposure to a peptide therapeutics market expected to grow above 15% a year and PolyPeptide's involvement in over one third of global Phase III peptide programs could support that turnaround, but the current loss base is still sizeable.
    • Analysts talk about profit margins moving from roughly a 9.4% loss today to an 8.7% profit in three years, yet the latest half year EPS of a €0.80 loss shows that the profit swing they expect has not started to show up in reported numbers.
    • Bulls also point to revenue growth forecasts of about 14.5% a year, which sit against the reality that net income excluding extra items for H1 2025 remains a €26.5 million loss, so investors have to decide how much weight to put on forecasts compared with the current loss run rate.
Have a look at how bullish investors connect these forecasts to a potential turnaround in profitability and where they think the upside could come from next. 🐂 PolyPeptide Group Bull Case

P/S Of 2.4x And CHF24.30 Price Versus DCF Fair Value Of CHF30.10

  • PolyPeptide is described as trading on a P/S of about 2.4x, below a European Life Sciences industry average of roughly 3.0x and a peer average of about 3.6x, with the current share price of CHF24.30 sitting under both a DCF fair value of CHF30.10 and an analyst price target of CHF34.99.
  • Bears counter that even with this discount, high share price volatility over the past three months and the extended loss history mean the lower multiple could be compensation for risk rather than an obvious bargain.
    • The risk summary flags materially higher volatility than the Swiss market and a multi year loss trend, which can justify investors asking for a lower multiple despite forecasts of 14.5% annual revenue growth.
    • On the other hand, supporters of the bearish view can point to the trailing 12 month €34.7 million loss as evidence that profitability has not yet caught up with either the DCF fair value of CHF30.10 or the analyst target of CHF34.99 that sit above today’s CHF24.30 price.
